ideal_must_detect.matched_text: a contracted network engineer on night shifts at the Newcastle site, born 03/1979, carries device tag RZ-14-88 and is paid to an account ending 4421
purge DOB and partial account references from ticketing systems
restrict employment/health fields to HR platforms

Under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), personal information includes information or an opinion about an identified or reasonably identifiable individual; under the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) and ASIC guidance, MNPI is information not generally available that would be expected to materially affect financial products.
Generic demographics: phrases like “staff aged in their 30s” or “regional NSW personnel” in guidance notes are non‑identifying and acceptable under our privacy threshold.

must_detect.matched_text:
To: Board Secretariat, Silvergum Technologies Limited
From: Legal & Compliance (legal@silvergum.com.au)
Date: 31 May 2026
Subject: Privacy Act and MNPI controls update for ASIC/ASX context

Under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), personal information includes information or an opinion about an identified or reasonably identifiable individual; under the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) and ASIC guidance, MNPI is information not generally available that would be expected to materially affect financial products.
Employment records: rosters and shift allocations are stored by position and site only (e.g., “support technician – Brisbane depot”); no DOBs, home addresses, or medical details are exported to operations reports.
Health data: EAP utilisation is reported in aggregate (e.g., three staff in Perth this quarter) and not linked to identities; staff may use the public well‑being helpline 1300 000 000, which is not a company contact.
Credit and onboarding: supplier forms capture entity details and are validated against public registers; any ABN-like placeholder “00 000 000 000” in draft screenshots is illustrative only; no consumer credit files are accessed.
Transactions and disclosure: the contract renewal noted in the April operations pack was disclosed on ASX on 15 April and forms part of our continuous disclosure history; any “pipeline” references here relate to already‑announced tenders and are not MNPI.
Intentional PII example for remediation: to resolve a payroll discrepancy, HR recorded that a contracted network engineer on night shifts at the Newcastle site, born 03/1979, carries device tag RZ-14-88 and is paid to an account ending 4421; this combination is quasi‑identifying and must be quarantined to HR systems with role‑based access only.
Device logs elsewhere list only asset classes (e.g., “laptop model AU-2021”) without user linkage; the generic string “ID: 123-ABC” in training slides is not an operational identifier.
Finance governance: the current draft board paper describes an internal product refresh without forecasts or revenue figures and, standing alone, is not price‑sensitive under ASX Listing Rule 3.1.
Generic demographics: phrases like “staff aged in their 30s” or “regional NSW personnel” in guidance notes are non‑identifying and acceptable under our privacy threshold.
